speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you, Deborah. Thank you. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then-- and then whenever you finish, we'll do your pictures and these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we have 30 minutes for this tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to save at least 10 minutes for those things--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I won't talk for 20 minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I just don't want you to feel--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned on the last tape-- and I want you to feel comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't want to impose any time limitations--

speaker: interviewer
text: We are-- we're-- we're happy to hear your story--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I thank you. But I think you guys would get bored-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I sometimes don't go back in my mind. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about when you came to the United States, in 1965.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Now? Oh, well, it-- it wasn't easy to say goodbye. I lived 15 years in Australia. And I have extremely good memories about Australia, about Melbourne, because we had very close friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as a rule, I think most of survivors, they gather with people that have the same past, the same experience. We always seem to find people that lived the same life or lost their families. So this is like a new family. Now you-- you make a new family, you meet friends that you lived in Europe with, and from Europe to Australia. I only had a couple of new friends, Italian friends-- my neighbors-- which I was also sorry to leave. And we still in touch with them. It just that it seemed like maybe it's not fair. It was a big, big decision to pack up and leave. But I don't know. It was economically-- and also with family, and my children never had any grandparents. And this aunt, she was so wonderful. She was like-- really like my mother. And my-- my uncle was a sweetheart. They were both very concerned. And they kept an apartment for months and months in their own building. They owned a little building, just a few apartments on North Spaulding, near Santa Monica Boulevard. And all I heard is every time, we have a vacancy. We're not gonna rent it out, because you need where to stay with your two kids, which was very generous-- very nice of them. But for some reason, it took forever. And I don't know. We really didn't push-- to rush-- leave. I registered with the American consulate when I was pregnant with Sylvia. That was 1960 my child was born. And I didn't leave until '65. So it seems that I never really insist on running away. I liked it. I really was very fond of my friends, of my life in Australia. And we spoke English by then. That was another reason that if I wouldn't-- if I had to learn another language to come to another country, Jack would never agree to go. But-- so it's a different-- Australians are a little bit different English to America. But that wasn't a big problem-- as long as it's basic letters of language, the same customs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were amazed. When we came here, my girls were little. One was 4, going on 5, and one was 12 and a 1/2. They said, Mommy, all the products that we're used to in Australia, you can buy here-- all the cereals, all the everything, all the drinks. It was just like an extension to the kids, which was OK, because it's not an easy-- any move is a big transition in your life. And even if you move from San Diego to Los Angeles, it would be hard, you know? So I think it was a big decision. But when we came, we found-- again, we found friends which we knew from Europe, and very close friends of my husband. And of course, my aunt and uncle-- they were wonderful-- kept an apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't even go and buy used furniture. She bought brand new bedroom, and a stove, and a refrigerator, which I think about that was beautiful from. She didn't want me to feel that I-- like second-hand some, which wouldn't be a crime. But she prepared a cute apartment. The only thing-- it was a one bedroom. So the girls slept in the bedroom, and we slept in the living room on a sofa bed-- me and Jack-- from the beginning. But it was-- everything was sparkling clean and brand new-- a kitchenette. Everything she bought and put in for us. And she was just as wonderful as she was when I came when she married me. You see, so she married me, and I came. And she felt responsible. And when Jack started working, he would say, well, you kept the apartment vacant for us. I mean, you're losing rent. Don't worry about. Once you start working, you can pay me the rent. She didn't want him to feel that-- she wouldn't even take in the rent. But that was so-- for him to feel that he's a little bit more independent-- so once he started working for this company, for children's wear, we paid the rent like tenants. And a year later, we start to look for a two bedroom. Because it wasn't that convenient. Mind you, it was easy to clean up. There was only one bedroom, and the living room-- we closed the sofa bed. And I started to look for work. So it was-- in the beginning, I could see Jack is missing his cultural work, his theater. He was working in the Yiddish theater as an amateur. He didn't get paid, but it was a great hobby. He loved it. And he missed it. And I was missing my close friends. I started to get a little bit social, but it wasn't easy. So when people invited me, the only thing I could do in the tiny apartment-- just a cup of coffee. I invited back. This way, I reciprocate and made friends. Because I believe in life there's no one way street. [LAUGHS] You have to be nice to people, and people are nice to you. And so it was not easy, but we managed. We missed it. And I decided, once I can manage to save money and go back, just to go back, I'll get it out of my system. Until then, I'll be-- I missed it more than Poland, more than-- and because this was my life, my married life. My children were born there. I went through diapering. I went through all these things. And I wanted to go back to Australia-- not to move back, but just to go back. But until then, which was seven years later, we went back for the trip, we tried to work. So I found a job. But that was much later. Because when I first came, I wasn't well. And all of a sudden, I found out I was hemorrhaging. And it was devastating. It was terribly, terribly unfortunate. And I was thinking, OK. I managed to have two daughters. We can't afford a bigger family, so might as well do it if I have to. But I didn't have any health insurance. And I had no way. And I didn't want to impose on my aunt to pay for major surgery.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember there was some kind of a-- not a public hospital, but they see the sign out on Beverly Boulevard-- was like they helped people that couldn't afford it. And I filled in some papers. Then they told me I have to be six months residence. So I went through a lot of suffering until I was actually a resident of California-- to do this surgery.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I still believed in those days, a lot of unnecessary hysterectomies were performed. I-- I don't regret it anymore, but a lot of my friends, we talked about it later on. There was a lot of things done, that right away, instead of helping someway-- but now, medicine is advanced. Medicine changes. That was it. That was unfortunate. But after I recovered-- and I was very young. I was only 37, going on 38. That is young to go through major surgery. But I recovered. I found a job as a saleslady. And because I spoke English-- I was never a saleslady in Australia. I worked in a-- in a-- as a-- in a tailor-- finishing factory. But here, I went to the Home Silk Shop, which was on La Cienega and 3rd. And the boss of the Home Silk Shop-- doesn't exist anymore-- he was very understanding. Maybe it was to his benefit, because he paid very little. But for some reason, he did employ new arrivals-- most of them-- especially if one that speak English-- that was an advantage. 'Cause I saw people that had hardly understood the language. And they gave me a job. The only problem was my child was only five years old, and they wanted me to work full time. So because I spoke the language, because I could influence my boss somehow, he told me, look. Let's try. Let's say, work till 4 PM-- if it will work. And then we'll see what we can do. And in those days, that was 1966 already, there was no Sunday, seven day week. Later on, I-- I remember when it started. Sears-- Sears and Roebuck were the very first one to open up seven days a week. First, it was around Christmas-- between Thanksgiving and Christmas. The stores started to open. And then everybody was open then. And when they opened on Sunday, he wanted me to work Sunday. Now this was the only time I had with my kids. So I begged him. I need a job. I wanna work, but please try to do something.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, well, come in every other Sunday just for a few hours. Because I must say, to my benefit, I was capable to master a cash register. Even if I was a saleslady, if somebody was missing, they'll put me on this department-- this. I-- I never said no.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in a job, even now, this is very important, even if you don't understand it. So I said, show me. If I know how, I'll do it. And I did. So then I worked-- helped the cashier. I helped anything that they gave me. And I-- I sold, because we all had to write down the sales. So I were-- they were pleased with me. And because they were pleased with me, they gave me my conditions. So I skipped one Sunday or the other. And then Jack started to work on his own. And he really couldn't work by himself. He was afraid that people steal. When you're alone, you're busy with a customer, somebody else will rip you off. And that's a shame, 'cause that's your profit.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he started to convince me, come with me one weekend. Just come. How can I come with you the weekend? I have to go Sunday to work. That was a little bit tense for the whole family-- for my daughters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dad goes to this swap meet. Mama has to work Sunday. I mean, now, when I think about it, we're talking about children that have-- that become independent, or have where the-- the family is important. You've got to watch what-- who the friends are and what they do. Well, I was lucky, because the girls were good girls. Especially Dinah was a built-in babysitter. She used to hate it. She used to tell me OK. I'm eight years older. So you waited so long so that I can watch my sister. But when we came here, she was already in junior high. And the little one started preschool. So she used to say herself, she says, Mom, I have homework, and my sister is just starting school. I'll keep an eye on her. You can work. And she encouraged me to go out of the house to take on a job-- my older daughter. And so for a while, I help part time. I went on a Sunday with Jack, and then I realized that he really needs me, that we both should work together.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in 9-- 10 years work-- I worked at Home Silk Shop-- from '66 to '76. In 1976 I quit. I resigned.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they really-- they were sorry to see me go. Because I received a bonus, which was enough for me to go to Australia. It's something like profit sharing, or some kind of a program my boss had for the workers. And-- and it was-- he couldn't even stop it, because it went into the bank in my name. So I could collect it. So I said to Jack then, I said, look. If we made it, and I could get money, it'll go into a melting pot. Let's go and visit our friends. So we saved a little more, and I went just with Jack. We left the girls here. We left the girls with my family-- with cousins that live in San Diego now. Well, Dinah was already a big girl, and she was very independent.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then I start working with Jack. And I worked until he gave it up-- until 1990-- so actually, from 7-- 1976 till 1990. May 1990, that was the last time we packed up.

speaker: interviewee
text: We gave it up-- literally gave it up, because we couldn't even sell the merchandise. And then we went to Israel to visit this daughter that lived there in Israel. And a year later, she moved back here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, what really happened is Sylvia started, the younger one start to become orthodox. And so she met this young man. And I don't know if this is important, but we have Sephardim and Ashkenazi. And we are from the Ashkenazi side. So my daughter never knew anything about Sephardim. Well, my son-in-law is from Turkish decent. He's a Sephard. And his parents came-- his parents-- American born. His grandparents moved here, I think. He's already third generation.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he's very American, but Sephardic with Sephardic customs. And so why I'm saying it is because she learned something else. It was a new experience. And they both went to study in Israel-- not together.

speaker: interviewee
text: She wanted to go to a girls school. And she never finished college here, but she decided to go. And she promised-- to let her go for one year. We had a-- a-- a promise that she will come back, and that she's really-- just want the experience of being in Jerusalem one year. And this was-- she was only 17 years old. And I thought, it's a good experience for her. We'll let her go. And then while she was there, and he was in a boys yeshiva, they both decide to get married. So we were upset. And his parents live here. So they said, no way. If-- you guys come back here. So they did come back, and that was a problem, because he-- he went on a one way ticket. Anyway, they came back. They got married. And they stayed only about two or three weeks. And they made aliyah as husband and wife, and went back to Israel on a one way ticket. And so they stayed there. And then my granddaughter was born in 1986. And now she--

speaker: interviewer
text: --anything in the future that you want to say about--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the-- you have-- you have such a good story. It's so--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you talk so well, that the time-- Yeah. --just flies by. This is--

speaker: interviewer
text: --one of the fastest interviews I've ever--

speaker: interviewer
text: --experienced. I feel like we-- we just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my goodness. You guys are tired.

speaker: interviewer
text: It went. I'm-- I'm telling you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you were just finished telling me when--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, she was born in 1986. And since both grandparents don't speak Hebrew, my-- my daughter and son-in-law spoke English at home, and Hebrew in the streets, Hebrew in the business. She worked in an office. And Sylvia was very capable, since she knew English and Hebrew perfect. And she worked computers. She always found a job as a secretary, and always was appreciate on her job, and my son-in-law. But life was hard. And we traveled. We-- we managed to visit often. So the granddaughter knew about us a lot. So when we first came, she was hardly speaking. She would say English words, but she was thinking in Hebrew, which was so cute. But whenever she said something and I didn't understand, my daughter would say, speak English, because your grandparents don't understand. But then they moved back here. So we have her, and we're very happy. And I-- I would love to go back to Israel as a tourist, but I'm so glad both my daughters lived here. Also, Dinah was away, 'cause she went to college up north. And so she lived ten years in-- in-- near San Francisco. So the-- the-- the end is that both daughters live in Southern California. We see each other often. And now we're a family again. I would like to say, as far as my-- I am an optimist. There are moments when I am-- I'm really missing my childhood. I see some moments where I was deprived. Because sometimes I see the abilities that each adult feels that has-- that weren't developed. I was only 12 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows? Maybe I would be an educator. Maybe I would have some qualities. Because I was pretty bright.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some of my family members say, your memory is good. You-- you-- for a sixth grader, you were OK in reading and writing. I write very well English. I [LAUGHS] very seldom make mistakes. And so it sometimes feels what I miss, and what my parents never managed to see. What I am sorry sometimes that I see where I was-- where I was-- how hurt it is that I have children. My daughter is older than my own mother was when she left me-- when I left her, or when we never saw each other. But there is one optimistic thought that I want to share with you. I always convinced that my-- this was like a-- a must. Because I never saw my mother old and grey, or my father sick and dead, I always believed that they're listening to me now that they up there. And when I was pregnant, I decided to give Jack the privilege, because he had no family. Nobody survived. Dinah is named after his mother. My first born I wanted to name after his family, because he had no one. And Sylvia is my mother. And it's interesting, they are so much-- whoever met Dinah-- which is really weird-- it-- it boggles my mind-- that if she ran into somebody as a child in Melbourne that remembered his parents from Lodz, they would say, you must be Lewin's daughter. You're so much like your grandma. And Sylvia is very much like my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what I see is a new generation formed in memory, in the picture-- in the-- in the-- in the-- not just in memory, but in the-- in the shape of the people that I lost. So I didn't lose them. These are my daughters, and this is my mother, and this Jack's mother. This is my attitude.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just-- I-- I don't know. Maybe I'll live another 10 years, maybe not. What I achieve now is I-- I try to quiet in my-- in myself and say, let's have good memories. Let's think of nice things-- nice. I read those little cards that I was sent to camp, or that mailed at camp to me, to camp. Or the-- the ladies, I wish I would know where they are. I don't even know who these girls are-- signed first names-- hope, a lot of hope, a lot of inspiration. I think this is what it's all about, the Jewish-- the Jewish race-- the-- the Jews. We are continuing. We pick ourselves up, we go on. This is how I see it. My-- they kick us around. They-- they think even now with all the people that hate us, they don't believe it. I do. I believe we will exist. We will continue in my granddaughters' future, and all the young children's future. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sylvia, could you hold this up, and show me, and tell me a little bit about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. OK. During my years working in concentration camp, I was given a little gift. I don't remember the girl. I don't know if she's still around. She could be an old lady now. I don't know where abouts. She was very creative. Where she found the thread and these, that-- that it didn't fade away, and it didn't fall apart. It's made from rubber. It's like a plastic that we were wearing to protect ourselves from the mud that was coming out from the machines. And this has a safety pin. I have no idea. This is two little dogs--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want-- do you want to stop it?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's not going to work. We need the--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll just say on the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, here-- like, as I'm-- yeah. On-- on the left you see a couple of doggies. It-- it's supposed to be a brooch. It's made out of a kind of a plastic or rubber, which was a uniform or a apron that I was wearing for work. And one of the girls that worked with me was very creative. She made it into a brooch. Where she found tools and stuff, I don't know, but it was very precious.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this rubber was from the concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was-- she got it off-- those aprons were protecting us from the mud and stuff that came from the factories, from the machines-- protection from machinery. And this-- this was how she got the thread and that-- that little string. I don't know. But that came-- that's from the camp, the one--

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been '43-- 19-- this was my birthday gift. And next to it is a medal. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just say this is medal that I'm--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a medal that a girl pulled off a belt from the soldiers-- the ladies-- the ladies that were wearing uniforms in camp when the liberation came-- and they tried to grab souvenirs. One girl gave me this as a souvenir. It's supposed to be some kind of a membership of a ski club in Germany. Those Nazi ladies belonged there. And I kept it. It-- the colors are now very hard to tell. But this is my souvenirs from the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture shows my grandmother, my mother, and a close friend, on a summer vacation. We-- I used to go every summer to visit my grandmother. And this was a very small town. This was in like a resort where the mountains and trees were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had a photograph with me in camp. I kept it all the time. I think this looks like 1937-- summer of '37 or '38.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left side is a close girlfriend. She lived in that town. And this is my mother. Next to it is my grandmother; and me with my little dog.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll-- We'll do this all together.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, during the war, in 1941, we were a group of close girlfriends. We used to gather quietly, and we took a picture. We had one friend, one boyfriend, that was a good photographer. And he took that picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I exactly don't remember all the names. But right in the middle, it's me, in a winter coat with a little fur collar. And these are four other girlfriends which are gone now. None of them survived-- with the armband. And this was 1941. You're standing in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here I have a-- a piece of paper, which I don't know where my friends in camp-- and it has on it Ober Altstadt, 1944. This is a birthday note. And it says here it's signed by somebody-- Bronia. And there is a Paula. It's written in Polish. And it wishes me that soon I should be freed, and I should be together with my parents. And the war should be over. This-- these words, the way they are written, I cannot read them to you. Because it's in Polish. But it-- this is my birthday gift that some of the girls ripped open. This is another piece of paper, also from the same. I don't know whether they got a-- a book somewhere. They wrote, or they have tore out a piece of paper. And it says here also-- and souvenir to Regina Szwarcfeld, in-- in day of your birthday. And we hope that you'll be freed soon. And this says here also, December 1944. It's in Roman letters-- 12/1944. In the middle is a little gift. It's supposed to be like a premium for good work. And it's only 15 [NON-ENGLISH] or something-- 50-- 5-0. It says the value of 50 [NON-ENGLISH] whatever-- German money. It's supposed to be a premium from-- sort of like a reward for the work that I did in that camp. So they gave out those things. And I kept it as a souvenir.

speaker: interviewee
text: This one says here Gross-Rosen, but I never was in that camp. Our camp, Ober Altstadt, belonged to Gross-Rosen. So that's why this premium thing gives from that city, from Gross-Rosen. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture shows a few years ago when my aunt and uncle were still alive. This is here in Los Angeles, right in my house. These are the people-- this is the couple with one of my cousins. They were risking their life. They were in hiding in Belgium. They lived in Brussels. And they sent packages to me from 1942 to '43.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. And Mrs. Gold-- Samuel Gold and Yeti Gold. And they both are gone now. They're both deceased now. They died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say in 1980, around that time, because she died in 1980. This was just before she died. And uncle died a few years ago. But he lived to be over 90.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're good, right? OK. And, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Lee, Perla, and Sylvia. This is my son-in-law, my granddaughter, and my younger daughter. This was taken here in my dining room, after they came back from Israel, in 1991.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait until Mike tells you OK-- with your granddaughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken on Mother's Day, 1992, in a kosher restaurant with my granddaughter and my husband, Jack. It-- I think we have different pictures later on, but this is one of the important pictures for me.
